title: Beta diversity
text:
2 Unsupervised analysis

2.1 Community dissimilarity
This document presents the results of a microbiota project, focusing on beta diversity analysis. Specifically, in this section, we employed Principal Coordinates Analysis (PCoA) to assess community dissimilarity among microbiota samples.

Taxonomic level: species
Dissimilarity index: bray
Group variable: diet
Multiple testing correction: fdr
Statistical test Wilcoxon

2.3 Table of the top findings

2.3.0.1 Paired treatment between timepoint(1 and 2)

Multiple testing correction: paired fdr
Statistical test: Wilcoxon

title: Beta diversity
text:
3 Supervised analysis

3.1 Permanova analysis

Dissimilarity index: bray
Group variable: diet

Interpretation

Homogeneity < 0.05: The assumption of homogeneity of variance is violated (i.e., the variances are significantly different across groups).
Homogeneity ≥ 0.05: The assumption of homogeneity of variance is not violated (i.e., the variances are similar across groups).

title: DAA Taxa
text:
Analysis information:

Method: maaslin3
Taxonomic level: species, genus, family, phylum
Group variable: diet
Formula used: ~ diet * time + (1 | id)
Correction: Benjamini-Hochberg FDR adjustment
Detection threshold: 0.001
Prevalence threshold: 0.1

For a complete table of maaslin3 results, see Species, genus, phylum, family
The following tables lists all associations that pass MaAsLin 3’s significance threshold for joint or individual q-values, ordered by increasing individual q-value.














This plots contain a combined coefficient plot and heatmap of the most significant association between microbial features (listed on the y-axis) and different dietary conditions over time.
The coef plot shows the estimated effect sizes (β coefficients) for different microbial features.

Circles (○) represent associations based on abundance.
Triangles (△) represent associations based on prevalence.

The purple color represents the significance level (FDR-adjusted p-value) for abundance. The teal color represents the significance level (FDR-adjusted p-value) for prevalence.Darker colors indicate more significant associations.Error bars show uncertainty in the estimates.
The heatmap summarizes how different microbes are affected. Blue/Red coloring represents the effect sizes:

Dark blue: Strong negative association.
Light blue: Weak negative association.
Light red: Weak positive association.
Dark red: Strong positive association.
Gray (NA): No data.

Asterisks () indicate significant associations: - (p < 0.1) - ** (p < 0.01)





taxa_image18





















Box plots are used for categorical data abundance associations. Grids are used for categorical data prevalence associations. Data points plotted are after filtering, normalization, and transformation so that the scale in the plot is the scale that was used in fitting.

title: Bacteroidetes/Firmicutes ratio
text:
Taxonomic level: Phylum
Group variable: diet
Multiple testing correction: fdr
Statistical test Wilcoxon









Multiple testing correction: fdr
Statistical test: Wilcoxon







The B/F ratio was slightly lower in the rice group (mean = 0.29) compared to the oat group (mean = 0.32). The fold change (log2FC = -0.12) suggests a small decrease in the ratio for the rice group. However, this difference was not statistically significant (p.adj = 0.410), indicating no strong evidence of a baseline difference between the two diet groups.




Multiple testing correction: fdr
Statistical test: Wilcoxon







The B/F ratio was identical between the oat and rice groups (mean = 0.33 for both) after treatment. The fold change (log2FC = -0.02) indicates minimal difference. The p-value (p.adj = 0.680) confirms that there is no statistically significant difference in the B/F ratio between the two diet groups post-treatment.
















Multiple testing correction: fdr
Statistical test: paired r test







In the oat diet group, the B/F ratio slightly increased from 0.32 to 0.33 (log2FC = 0.28, p = 0.800), indicating no significant change over time.
In the rice diet group, the B/F ratio increased from 0.29 to 0.33 (log2FC = 1.07, p = 0.370), but this change was not statistically significant.
Hence, B/F ratio remained relatively stable over time for both diet groups, with no significant shifts from before to after treatment.






Multiple testing correction: FDR
Statistical test: paired Wilcoxon
Formula used: ~ diet * timepoint + (1 | id)







The Intercept is highly significant (Estimate = -1.93, p = 8.67e-8), suggesting a clear baseline effect before treatment.
The dietrice term, representing the difference between the rice and oat groups, is not significant (Estimate = -0.51, p = 0.286).
The time term, indicating any overall time effect, is also not significant (Estimate = -0.02, p = 0.907).
The interaction between diet and time (dietrice:time) shows a non-significant effect (Estimate = 0.22, p = 0.428), indicating that the effect of diet over time is not statistically different for rice vs. oat.

Multiple testing correction: fdr

The B/F ratio was slightly lower in the rice group (mean = 0.29) compared to the oat group (mean = 0.32). The fold change (log2FC = -0.12) suggests a small decrease in the ratio for the rice group. However, this difference was not statistically significant (p.adj = 0.410), indicating no strong evidence of a baseline difference between the two diet groups.




Multiple testing correction: fdr
Statistical test: Wilcoxon







The B/F ratio was identical between the oat and rice groups (mean = 0.33 for both) after treatment. The fold change (log2FC = -0.02) indicates minimal difference. The p-value (p.adj = 0.680) confirms that there is no statistically significant difference in the B/F ratio between the two diet groups post-treatment.
















Multiple testing correction: fdr
Statistical test: paired r test







In the oat diet group, the B/F ratio slightly increased from 0.32 to 0.33 (log2FC = 0.28, p = 0.800), indicating no significant change over time.
In the rice diet group, the B/F ratio increased from 0.29 to 0.33 (log2FC = 1.07, p = 0.370), but this change was not statistically significant.

Multiple testing correction: FDR
Statistical test: paired Wilcoxon
Formula used: ~ diet * timepoint + (1 | id)







The Intercept is highly significant (Estimate = -1.93, p = 8.67e-8), suggesting a clear baseline effect before treatment.
The dietrice term, representing the difference between the rice and oat groups, is not significant (Estimate = -0.51, p = 0.286).
The time term, indicating any overall time effect, is also not significant (Estimate = -0.02, p = 0.907).
The interaction between diet and time (dietrice:time) shows a non-significant effect (Estimate = 0.22, p = 0.428), indicating that the effect of diet over time is not statistically different for rice vs. oat.
